key_points: Figures can have both line and rotational symmetry (the 'Common Ground')
Translational symmetry involves sliding a pattern without rotating or flipping it
Tessellations (tilings) are created using combined symmetry operations
Point symmetry is a special case where a figure matches itself after a $180^{\circ}$ rotation
examples:
Both symmetries: Square (4 lines, Order 4), Regular Hexagon (6 lines, Order 6) 

[Image of lines of symmetry in regular polygons]
Translational: Honeycomb patterns, wallpaper designs, brick walls
Alphabet check: Letter 'Z' has rotational symmetry but no line symmetry
formulas: For a shape to tile $\rightarrow \text{Interior angle sum at vertex} = 360^{\circ}$
Symmetry in coordinates $\rightarrow (x, y)$ reflected over y-axis becomes $(-x, y)$
exam_tips: Identify the 'Common Ground' by listing both the number of lines and the order of rotation
In complex designs (like Rangoli), look for a repeating basic unit
Regular pentagons do not have rotational symmetry of order 2 ($180^{\circ}$)
Practice visualizing the 'net' effect of multiple transformations (reflection + rotation)
